  • 2-2) Top K Frequent Elements (LC 347) — Min-Heap with Frequency
  • 2-3) Merge K Sorted Lists (LC 23) — Min-Heap

Priority Queue (PQ) Data Structure

Overview

Priority Queue is an abstract data type that operates similar to a regular queue but with an added priority element. Elements are served based on their priority rather than the order they were added.

Key Properties

  • Time Complexity:
    • Insert: O(log n)
    • Delete max/min: O(log n)
    • Get max/min: O(1)
    • Heapify: O(n)
  • Space Complexity: O(n)
  • Core Idea: Elements with higher priority are served before elements with lower priority
  • When to Use: When you need to process elements based on priority/order, not just insertion time

Implementation

  • Usually implemented using Binary Heap (min-heap or max-heap)
  • Can also use balanced BST or Fibonacci heap for advanced operations
  • Python: heapq module (min-heap by default)
  • Java: PriorityQueue class (min-heap by default)

References

  • Python heapq documentation
  • Java PriorityQueue
  • Priority Queue Implementation Notes

Problem Categories

Pattern 1: Top K Elements

  • Description: Finding K largest/smallest elements efficiently
  • Examples: LC 215, 347, 692, 973, 1985
  • Pattern: Use min-heap for K largest, max-heap for K smallest

Pattern 2: Merge K Sorted

  • Description: Merging multiple sorted sequences
  • Examples: LC 23, 88, 313, 373, 786
  • Pattern: Use PQ to track current smallest/largest from each sequence

Pattern 3: Scheduling & Intervals

  • Description: Task scheduling and interval processing
  • Examples: LC 253, 1094, 1353, 1834, 2402
  • Pattern: Sort by start time, use PQ for end times or priorities

Pattern 4: Sliding Window with Order

  • Description: Maintaining order statistics in sliding windows
  • Examples: LC 239, 480, 703, 1438, 2542
  • Pattern: Use PQ to track min/max in current window

Pattern 5: Graph Algorithms

  • Description: Shortest path and MST algorithms
  • Examples: LC 743, 787, 1514, 1584, 1631
  • Pattern: Dijkstra’s algorithm, Prim’s algorithm

Pattern 6: Data Stream & Median

  • Description: Processing continuous data streams
  • Examples: LC 295, 346, 352, 703, 1825
  • Pattern: Two-heap technique for median, PQ for percentiles

Pattern 7: Greedy String/Sequence Building with Constraint

  • Description: Build a string/sequence greedily using the most frequent element, but skip it when adding it would violate a constraint (e.g., 3 consecutive same chars). Use a max-heap to always have the current most frequent element ready.
  • Examples: LC 1405 (Longest Happy String), LC 767 (Reorganize String), LC 621 (Task Scheduler), LC 358 (Rearrange String k Distance Apart)
  • Pattern: Max-heap ordered by count; on each step try the top element — if it violates the constraint, temporarily use the 2nd element, then put the 1st back
  • Key Trick: Two-case loop
    1. Case 1 — constraint violated: poll second, append it, decrement, re-add if > 0; then re-add first (it was NOT consumed)
    2. Case 2 — safe: append first, decrement, re-add if > 0

Pattern 8: PQ + Cooldown Queue (k-Distance Scheduling)

  • Description: Greedily pick the most frequent element from a max-heap, then lock it in a cooldown queue for k steps before it can be reused. This is the canonical pattern for “same element must be at least k distance apart” problems.
  • Examples: LC 358 (Rearrange String k Distance Apart), LC 621 (Task Scheduler), LC 767 (Reorganize String — k=2 special case)
  • Pattern: Max-heap picks next element; after use, element enters a cooldown queue with releaseTime = time + k; when time == releaseTime, element is moved back to the heap
  • Key Insight: PQ alone cannot track “last used position” — the cooldown queue acts as a k-slot delay line that automatically re-enables elements after k steps
  • When to Use:
    1. Problem says “same element at least k apart” or “cooldown of k”
    2. Need to greedily pick most frequent available element
    3. Elements cycle through available → used → cooling → available
  • Difference from Pattern 7: Pattern 7 checks a look-back window and swaps elements; Pattern 8 uses an explicit cooldown queue to enforce the distance, which is cleaner for variable k

Templates & Algorithms

Template Comparison Table

Template Type Use Case Heap Type Complexity When to Use
Top K Elements Find K largest/smallest Min/Max heap O(n log k) Fixed K selection
K-Way Merge Merge sorted lists Min heap O(n log k) Multiple sorted sources
Two Heaps Find median Min + Max heap O(log n) Stream median/percentile
Interval Scheduling Process intervals Min heap O(n log n) Meeting rooms, events
Graph Shortest Path Dijkstra’s Min heap O(E log V) Weighted graphs
Custom Priority Complex ordering Custom comparator O(log n) Multi-criteria sorting
Greedy + Constraint Build string avoiding consecutive repeats Max heap O(n log k) Reorganize/happy string
PQ + Cooldown Queue k-distance apart scheduling Max heap + Queue O(n log k) Rearrange k-dist, task scheduler

Template 1: Top K Elements Pattern

1# Python - Find K largest elements
2def topKElements(nums, k):
3    import heapq
4    
5    # Min heap of size k for k largest
6    min_heap = []
7    
8    for num in nums:
9        heapq.heappush(min_heap, num)
10        if len(min_heap) > k:
11            heapq.heappop(min_heap)
12    
13    return min_heap  # Contains k largest elements
14
15# With custom key for frequency
16def topKFrequent(nums, k):
17    from collections import Counter
18    import heapq
19    
20    count = Counter(nums)
21    # Use negative count for max heap effect
22    return heapq.nlargest(k, count.keys(), key=count.get)

1// Java - Top K elements with frequency
2public int[] topKFrequent(int[] nums, int k) {
3    Map<Integer, Integer> map = new HashMap<>();
4    for (int n : nums) {
5        map.put(n, map.getOrDefault(n, 0) + 1);
6    }
7    
8    // Min heap based on frequency
9    PriorityQueue<Integer> pq = new PriorityQueue<>(
10        (a, b) -> map.get(a) - map.get(b)
11    );
12    
13    for (int key : map.keySet()) {
14        pq.add(key);
15        if (pq.size() > k) {
16            pq.poll();
17        }
18    }
19    
20    int[] result = new int[k];
21    for (int i = k - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
22        result[i] = pq.poll();
23    }
24    return result;
25}

Template 2: K-Way Merge Pattern

1# Python - Merge K sorted lists
2def mergeKSortedLists(lists):
3    import heapq
4    
5    min_heap = []
6    
7    # Initialize with first element from each list
8    for i, lst in enumerate(lists):
9        if lst:
10            heapq.heappush(min_heap, (lst[0], i, 0))
11    
12    result = []
13    while min_heap:
14        val, list_idx, elem_idx = heapq.heappop(min_heap)
15        result.append(val)
16        
17        # Add next element from same list
18        if elem_idx + 1 < len(lists[list_idx]):
19            next_val = lists[list_idx][elem_idx + 1]
20            heapq.heappush(min_heap, (next_val, list_idx, elem_idx + 1))
21    
22    return result

1// Java - Merge K sorted arrays
2public int[] mergeKSortedArrays(int[][] arrays) {
3    PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>(
4        (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]  // Compare values
5    );
6    
7    int totalSize = 0;
8    // Initialize PQ with first element from each array
9    for (int i = 0; i < arrays.length; i++) {
10        if (arrays[i].length > 0) {
11            pq.offer(new int[]{arrays[i][0], i, 0});
12            totalSize += arrays[i].length;
13        }
14    }
15    
16    int[] result = new int[totalSize];
17    int idx = 0;
18    
19    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
20        int[] curr = pq.poll();
21        result[idx++] = curr[0];
22        
23        int arrIdx = curr[1];
24        int elemIdx = curr[2];
25        
26        if (elemIdx + 1 < arrays[arrIdx].length) {
27            pq.offer(new int[]{
28                arrays[arrIdx][elemIdx + 1], 
29                arrIdx, 
30                elemIdx + 1
31            });
32        }
33    }
34    
35    return result;
36}

Template 3: Two Heaps Pattern (Median Finding)

1# Python - Find median from data stream
2class MedianFinder:
3    def __init__(self):
4        self.small = []  # Max heap (negate values)
5        self.large = []  # Min heap
6    
7    def addNum(self, num):
8        import heapq
9        
10        # Add to max heap (small values)
11        heapq.heappush(self.small, -num)
12        
13        # Balance: ensure max of small <= min of large
14        if self.small and self.large and (-self.small[0] > self.large[0]):
15            val = -heapq.heappop(self.small)
16            heapq.heappush(self.large, val)
17        
18        # Balance sizes
19        if len(self.small) > len(self.large) + 1:
20            val = -heapq.heappop(self.small)
21            heapq.heappush(self.large, val)
22        if len(self.large) > len(self.small) + 1:
23            val = heapq.heappop(self.large)
24            heapq.heappush(self.small, -val)
25    
26    def findMedian(self):
27        if len(self.small) > len(self.large):
28            return -self.small[0]
29        if len(self.large) > len(self.small):
30            return self.large[0]
31        return (-self.small[0] + self.large[0]) / 2.0

1// Java - Two heaps for median
2class MedianFinder {
3    private PriorityQueue<Integer> small;  // Max heap
4    private PriorityQueue<Integer> large;  // Min heap
5    
6    public MedianFinder() {
7        small = new PriorityQueue<>(Collections.reverseOrder());
8        large = new PriorityQueue<>();
9    }
10    
11    public void addNum(int num) {
12        small.offer(num);
13        
14        // Balance property
15        if (!small.isEmpty() && !large.isEmpty() && 
16            small.peek() > large.peek()) {
17            large.offer(small.poll());
18        }
19        
20        // Size property
21        if (small.size() > large.size() + 1) {
22            large.offer(small.poll());
23        }
24        if (large.size() > small.size() + 1) {
25            small.offer(large.poll());
26        }
27    }
28    
29    public double findMedian() {
30        if (small.size() > large.size()) {
31            return small.peek();
32        }
33        if (large.size() > small.size()) {
34            return large.peek();
35        }
36        return (small.peek() + large.peek()) / 2.0;
37    }
38}

Template 4: Interval Scheduling Pattern

1# Python - Meeting rooms (minimum rooms needed)
2def minMeetingRooms(intervals):
3    import heapq
4    
5    if not intervals:
6        return 0
7    
8    # Sort by start time
9    intervals.sort(key=lambda x: x[0])
10    
11    # Min heap to track end times
12    heap = []
13    heapq.heappush(heap, intervals[0][1])
14    
15    for i in range(1, len(intervals)):
16        # If current meeting starts after earliest end
17        if intervals[i][0] >= heap[0]:
18            heapq.heappop(heap)
19        
20        # Add current meeting end time
21        heapq.heappush(heap, intervals[i][1])
22    
23    return len(heap)

1// Java - Interval scheduling
2public int minMeetingRooms(int[][] intervals) {
3    if (intervals.length == 0) return 0;
4    
5    // Sort by start time
6    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
7    
8    // Min heap for end times
9    PriorityQueue<Integer> pq = new PriorityQueue<>();
10    pq.offer(intervals[0][1]);
11    
12    for (int i = 1; i < intervals.length; i++) {
13        // Room becomes free
14        if (intervals[i][0] >= pq.peek()) {
15            pq.poll();
16        }
17        pq.offer(intervals[i][1]);
18    }
19    
20    return pq.size();
21}

Template 5: Graph Shortest Path (Dijkstra)

1# Python - Dijkstra's algorithm with PQ
2def dijkstra(graph, start, end):
3    import heapq
4    
5    # Min heap: (distance, node)
6    pq = [(0, start)]
7    distances = {start: 0}
8    visited = set()
9    
10    while pq:
11        curr_dist, curr_node = heapq.heappop(pq)
12        
13        if curr_node in visited:
14            continue
15        visited.add(curr_node)
16        
17        if curr_node == end:
18            return curr_dist
19        
20        for neighbor, weight in graph[curr_node]:
21            if neighbor not in visited:
22                new_dist = curr_dist + weight
23                if neighbor not in distances or new_dist < distances[neighbor]:
24                    distances[neighbor] = new_dist
25                    heapq.heappush(pq, (new_dist, neighbor))
26    
27    return -1  # Path not found

1// Java - Dijkstra with Priority Queue
2public int dijkstra(Map<Integer, List<int[]>> graph, int start, int end) {
3    // Min heap: [distance, node]
4    PriorityQueue<int[]> pq = new PriorityQueue<>(
5        (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]
6    );
7    
8    Map<Integer, Integer> distances = new HashMap<>();
9    Set<Integer> visited = new HashSet<>();
10    
11    pq.offer(new int[]{0, start});
12    distances.put(start, 0);
13    
14    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
15        int[] curr = pq.poll();
16        int currDist = curr[0];
17        int currNode = curr[1];
18        
19        if (visited.contains(currNode)) continue;
20        visited.add(currNode);
21        
22        if (currNode == end) return currDist;
23        
24        if (graph.containsKey(currNode)) {
25            for (int[] edge : graph.get(currNode)) {
26                int neighbor = edge[0];
27                int weight = edge[1];
28                
29                if (!visited.contains(neighbor)) {
30                    int newDist = currDist + weight;
31                    if (!distances.containsKey(neighbor) || 
32                        newDist < distances.get(neighbor)) {
33                        distances.put(neighbor, newDist);
34                        pq.offer(new int[]{newDist, neighbor});
35                    }
36                }
37            }
38        }
39    }
40    
41    return -1;
42}

Template 6: Custom Priority Pattern

1# Python - Custom priority with multiple criteria
2class Task:
3    def __init__(self, name, priority, deadline):
4        self.name = name
5        self.priority = priority
6        self.deadline = deadline
7    
8    def __lt__(self, other):
9        # Higher priority first, then earlier deadline
10        if self.priority != other.priority:
11            return self.priority > other.priority
12        return self.deadline < other.deadline
13
14def processTasks(tasks):
15    import heapq
16    
17    heap = []
18    for task in tasks:
19        heapq.heappush(heap, task)
20    
21    result = []
22    while heap:
23        task = heapq.heappop(heap)
24        result.append(task.name)
25    
26    return result

1// Java - Custom comparator for complex ordering
2class Task {
3    String name;
4    int priority;
5    int deadline;
6    
7    Task(String name, int priority, int deadline) {
8        this.name = name;
9        this.priority = priority;
10        this.deadline = deadline;
11    }
12}
13
14public List<String> processTasks(List<Task> tasks) {
15    PriorityQueue<Task> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> {
16        // Higher priority first
17        if (a.priority != b.priority) {
18            return b.priority - a.priority;
19        }
20        // Earlier deadline first
21        return a.deadline - b.deadline;
22    });
23    
24    for (Task task : tasks) {
25        pq.offer(task);
26    }
27    
28    List<String> result = new ArrayList<>();
29    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
30        result.add(pq.poll().name);
31    }
32    
33    return result;
34}

Template 7: Greedy String Building with Consecutive Constraint

1// Java - Longest Happy String (LC 1405) / Reorganize String (LC 767)
2// IDEA: Max-heap by count; two-case loop:
3//   Case 1: top char would create 3 consecutive → use 2nd, put 1st back
4//   Case 2: safe → use top char directly
5// time = O((a+b+c) * log(3)) = O(n), space = O(1) heap size bounded by alphabet
6
7class ValCnt {
8    char val;
9    int cnt;
10    ValCnt(char val, int cnt) { this.val = val; this.cnt = cnt; }
11}
12
13public String longestDiverseString(int a, int b, int c) {
14    PriorityQueue<ValCnt> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((x, y) -> y.cnt - x.cnt);
15    if (a > 0) pq.add(new ValCnt('a', a));
16    if (b > 0) pq.add(new ValCnt('b', b));
17    if (c > 0) pq.add(new ValCnt('c', c));
18
19    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
20
21    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
22        ValCnt first = pq.poll();
23        int len = sb.length();
24
25        // Case 1: adding `first` would create 3 consecutive → use second instead
26        if (len >= 2
27                && sb.charAt(len - 1) == first.val
28                && sb.charAt(len - 2) == first.val) {
29
30            if (pq.isEmpty()) break;          // no alternative → stop
31
32            ValCnt second = pq.poll();        // use 2nd most frequent
33            sb.append(second.val);
34            second.cnt--;
35
36            if (second.cnt > 0) pq.add(second);
37            pq.add(first);                    // first was NOT used, put it back
38
39        // Case 2: safe to use the most frequent character
40        } else {
41            sb.append(first.val);
42            first.cnt--;
43            if (first.cnt > 0) pq.add(first);
44        }
45    }
46
47    return sb.toString();
48}

Key Observations:

  • Always greedily pick the most frequent (max-heap ensures this).
  • When the constraint is about to be violated, temporarily skip the top element and use the next — then put the top back unchanged.
  • first is only consumed in Case 2; in Case 1 it is re-inserted untouched.
  • Works for any “at most K consecutive” constraint by changing the look-back window check.

Variant: Reorganize String (LC 767) — at most 1 consecutive

1// Only Case 1 check changes: len >= 1 && sb.charAt(len-1) == first.val
2// Everything else is identical to the template above.

Template 8: PQ + Cooldown Queue (k-Distance Scheduling)

1// Java - Rearrange String k Distance Apart (LC 358)
2// IDEA: Max-heap picks most frequent available char;
3//       cooldown queue locks used chars for k steps.
4//
5// Flow: PQ → poll → append → cooldown.offer([char, releaseTime])
6//       when time == releaseTime → move back to PQ
7//
8// time = O(n log 26) = O(n), space = O(26) = O(1)
9
10public String rearrangeString(String s, int k) {
11    if (k <= 1) return s;
12
13    int[] freq = new int[26];
14    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
15        freq[c - 'a']++;
16    }
17
18    // Max-heap ordered by remaining frequency
19    PriorityQueue<Integer> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> freq[b] - freq[a]);
20    for (int i = 0; i < 26; i++) {
21        if (freq[i] > 0) pq.offer(i);
22    }
23
24    // Cooldown queue: [charIndex, remainingCount]
25    // Size reaches k → front element has cooled for k steps → re-enable
26    Queue<int[]> cooldown = new LinkedList<>();
27    StringBuilder res = new StringBuilder();
28
29    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
30        int idx = pq.poll();
31        res.append((char) ('a' + idx));
32        freq[idx]--;
33
34        // Enter cooldown with current remaining count
35        cooldown.offer(new int[]{idx, freq[idx]});
36
37        // Release from cooldown after k steps
38        if (cooldown.size() == k) {
39            int[] ready = cooldown.poll();
40            if (ready[1] > 0) {
41                pq.offer(ready[0]);  // re-add to heap
42            }
43        }
44    }
45
46    return res.length() == s.length() ? res.toString() : "";
47}

Key Observations:

  • The cooldown queue acts as a fixed-size delay line of length k. When its size reaches k, the oldest entry has waited exactly k steps and is ready.
  • If pq is empty but cooldown still has entries → impossible to place anything → return "".
  • Alternative cooldown approach: store [char, releaseTime] and check cooldown.peek()[1] == time instead of checking queue size. Both are equivalent.
  • This pattern generalizes: LC 621 (Task Scheduler) uses the same idea but counts idle slots; LC 767 is k=2 special case.

Comparison: Cooldown Queue vs Skip-and-Swap (Template 7)

Aspect Cooldown Queue (Template 8) Skip-and-Swap (Template 7)
Best for Variable k, large k Small k (k=2 or k=3)
Mechanism Explicit queue delays re-entry Look-back window + swap
Impossible detection pq.isEmpty() while cooldown non-empty N/A (stops when no option)
Cleaner for LC 358, LC 621 LC 1405, LC 767

Basic Operations

Python heapq Operations

1import heapq
2
3# Create heap (min-heap by default)
4heap = []
5
6# Push element
7heapq.heappush(heap, 5)
8
9# Pop smallest
10smallest = heapq.heappop(heap)
11
12# Push and pop in one operation
13val = heapq.heappushpop(heap, 3)  # Push 3, then pop smallest
14
15# Pop and push in one operation  
16val = heapq.heapreplace(heap, 3)  # Pop smallest, then push 3
17
18# Get smallest without removing
19smallest = heap[0] if heap else None
20
21# Convert list to heap in-place
22nums = [3, 1, 4, 1, 5]
23heapq.heapify(nums)  # O(n) time
24
25# Get n largest/smallest
26largest_k = heapq.nlargest(k, nums)
27smallest_k = heapq.nsmallest(k, nums)
28
29# Max heap trick (negate values)
30max_heap = []
31heapq.heappush(max_heap, -5)  # Push
32max_val = -heapq.heappop(max_heap)  # Pop

Java PriorityQueue Operations

1import java.util.*;
2
3// Create PQ (min-heap by default)
4PriorityQueue<Integer> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>();
5
6// Create max-heap
7PriorityQueue<Integer> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(Collections.reverseOrder());
8
9// Add elements
10minHeap.offer(5);
11minHeap.add(3);  // Same as offer
12
13// Remove and return smallest/largest
14Integer smallest = minHeap.poll();
15
16// Peek without removing
17Integer top = minHeap.peek();
18
19// Check if empty
20boolean isEmpty = minHeap.isEmpty();
21
22// Get size
23int size = minHeap.size();
24
25// Clear all elements
26minHeap.clear();
27
28// Custom comparator
29PriorityQueue<int[]> customPQ = new PriorityQueue<>(
30    (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]  // Compare first element
31);

Problems by Pattern

Pattern-Based Problem Tables

Top K Elements Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Kth Largest Element in an Array 215 Quick select or min-heap Medium
Top K Frequent Elements 347 Frequency map + heap Medium
Top K Frequent Words 692 Custom comparator Medium
K Closest Points to Origin 973 Distance calculation Medium
Find K Pairs with Smallest Sums 373 K-way merge pattern Medium
Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix 378 Binary search or heap Medium
Find K-th Smallest Pair Distance 719 Binary search + sliding Hard
K-th Smallest Prime Fraction 786 Binary search or heap Medium

Merge K Sorted Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Merge k Sorted Lists 23 K-way merge Hard
Merge Sorted Array 88 Two pointers Easy
Smallest Range Covering K Lists 632 Multi-pointer + heap Hard
Find K Pairs with Smallest Sums 373 Heap with pairs Medium
Super Ugly Number 313 Multiple pointers Medium

Scheduling & Interval Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Meeting Rooms II 253 Sort + min heap Medium
Task Scheduler 621 Greedy + counting Medium
Reorganize String 767 Max heap + greedy Medium
Car Pooling 1094 Timeline events Medium
Maximum Events That Can Be Attended 1353 Sort + greedy Medium
Single-Threaded CPU 1834 Two heaps Medium
Maximum Number of Tasks You Can Assign 2071 Binary search + greedy Hard

Sliding Window with Order Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Sliding Window Maximum 239 Deque or heap Hard
Sliding Window Median 480 Two heaps Hard
Kth Largest Element in a Stream 703 Min heap of size k Easy
Longest Continuous Subarray 1438 Two deques Medium
Maximum Score of a Good Subarray 1793 Monotonic stack Hard

Graph Algorithm Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Network Delay Time 743 Dijkstra’s Medium
Cheapest Flights Within K Stops 787 Modified Dijkstra Medium
Path with Maximum Probability 1514 Dijkstra variant Medium
Path With Minimum Effort 1631 Binary search + DFS/Dijkstra Medium
Min Cost to Connect All Points 1584 Prim’s MST Medium
Swim in Rising Water 778 Binary search or Dijkstra Hard
Reachable Nodes In Subdivided Graph 882 Dijkstra’s Hard

Data Stream & Median Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Find Median from Data Stream 295 Two heaps Hard
Moving Average from Data Stream 346 Queue Easy
Data Stream as Disjoint Intervals 352 TreeMap or heap Hard
Kth Largest Element in a Stream 703 Min heap Easy
Finding MK Average 1825 Multiset simulation Hard

Greedy + Consecutive Constraint Problems

Problem LC # Key Technique Difficulty
Longest Happy String 1405 Max heap + greedy two-case loop Medium
Reorganize String 767 Max heap + greedy (no adjacent same) Medium
Task Scheduler 621 Max heap + greedy cooldown Medium
Rearrange String k Distance Apart 358 Max heap + greedy (k-distance) Hard
Distant Barcodes 1054 Max heap + greedy (no adjacent same) Medium

Pattern Selection Strategy

1Problem Analysis Flowchart:
2
31. Do you need to find K largest/smallest elements?
4   ├── YES → Use Top K Elements pattern
5   │         ├── K largest → Min heap of size K
6   │         └── K smallest → Max heap of size K
7   └── NO → Continue to 2
8
92. Are you merging multiple sorted sequences?
10   ├── YES → Use K-Way Merge pattern
11   │         └── Track position in each sequence
12   └── NO → Continue to 3
13
143. Is it a scheduling/interval problem?
15   ├── YES → Use Interval Scheduling pattern
16   │         ├── Sort by start time
17   │         └── Use heap for end times
18   └── NO → Continue to 4
19
204. Do you need to maintain order in sliding window?
21   ├── YES → Use Sliding Window with Order
22   │         └── Consider deque vs heap trade-offs
23   └── NO → Continue to 5
24
255. Is it a graph shortest path problem?
26   ├── YES → Use Dijkstra pattern with PQ
27   │         └── Min heap with distances
28   └── NO → Continue to 6
29
306. Processing data stream for statistics?
31   ├── YES → Use Data Stream pattern
32   │         ├── Median → Two heaps
33   │         └── Top K → Fixed size heap
34   └── NO → Continue to 7
35
367. Build string/sequence with "no K consecutive same" constraint?
37   ├── YES → Use Greedy + Constraint pattern
38   │         ├── Max-heap ordered by frequency
39   │         ├── Case 1 (constraint violated): use 2nd element, put 1st back
40   │         └── Case 2 (safe): use 1st element
41   └── NO → Use Custom Priority pattern

Summary & Quick Reference

Complexity Quick Reference

Operation Time Complexity Space Notes
Insert O(log n) O(1) Heap rebalancing
Extract min/max O(log n) O(1) Heap rebalancing
Peek min/max O(1) O(1) Direct access
Build heap O(n) O(n) Bottom-up heapify
K-way merge O(n log k) O(k) k = number of lists
Top K elements O(n log k) O(k) Maintain heap of size k
Heap sort O(n log n) O(1) In-place sorting

Template Quick Reference

Template Pattern Key Code
Top K Min heap for K largest if len(heap) > k: heappop(heap)
K-Way Merge Track indices (val, list_idx, elem_idx)
Two Heaps Balance sizes if len(small) > len(large) + 1
Intervals Sort + heap sort by start, heap for end times
Dijkstra Min distance (distance, node) in heap
Custom Comparator __lt__ in Python, Comparator in Java
Greedy+Constraint Two-case loop case1: use 2nd, put 1st back; case2: use 1st

Common Patterns & Tricks

Heap Direction Trick

1# Python max heap using negation
2max_heap = []
3heapq.heappush(max_heap, -value)  # Push
4max_value = -heapq.heappop(max_heap)  # Pop

K-Size Maintenance

1# Maintain exactly k elements
2if len(heap) > k:
3    heapq.heappop(heap)
4# Now heap contains k largest (min-heap) or k smallest (max-heap)

Lazy Deletion Pattern

1# Mark as deleted without immediate removal
2deleted = set()
3while heap and heap[0] in deleted:
4    heapq.heappop(heap)

Two Heaps Balance

1# Keep sizes balanced within 1
2if len(small) > len(large) + 1:
3    large.push(small.pop())
4if len(large) > len(small) + 1:
5    small.push(large.pop())

Problem-Solving Steps

  1. Identify the Pattern

    • Is it about K elements? → Top K pattern
    • Multiple sorted sources? → K-way merge
    • Need median/percentiles? → Two heaps
    • Graph shortest path? → Dijkstra with PQ

  2. Choose Heap Type

    • K largest → Min heap of size K
    • K smallest → Max heap of size K
    • Median → Two heaps (max + min)

  3. Design the Element

    • Simple value or tuple?
    • What to compare? (value, index, custom)
    • Need to track source? (for merging)

  4. Handle Edge Cases

    • Empty heap checks
    • K > n scenarios
    • Duplicate elements
    • Custom comparator edge cases

Common Mistakes & Tips

🚫 Common Mistakes:

  • Using max heap for K largest (should use min heap)
  • Forgetting to maintain heap size ≤ K
  • Not handling empty heap before peek
  • Wrong comparator direction
  • Not considering duplicates in custom comparators
  • In greedy+constraint pattern: forgetting to re-add first back to PQ in Case 1 (it was NOT consumed)

✅ Best Practices:

  • Always check heap empty before peek/pop
  • Use tuples for complex comparisons
  • Maintain heap invariants after each operation
  • Consider space-time trade-offs for large K
  • Use heapify for batch initialization

Interview Tips

  1. Clarify Requirements

    • Ask about K relative to N
    • Confirm if duplicates are allowed
    • Check if online/offline algorithm needed

  2. Optimize for K

    • K small → Heap approach O(n log k)
    • K ≈ n → Quick select O(n) average
    • K = 1 → Simple scan O(n)

  3. Explain Trade-offs

    • Heap: Good for streaming, K << n
    • Sorting: Simple but O(n log n)
    • Quick select: Best average case but unstable

  4. Implementation Details

    • Python: heapq is min-heap only
    • Java: PriorityQueue with Comparator
    • Remember heap is not sorted internally

Advanced Techniques

Indexed Priority Queue

  • Allows decrease-key operation
  • Useful for Dijkstra optimization
  • Track element positions in heap

Fibonacci Heap

  • O(1) amortized insert, decrease-key
  • O(log n) extract-min
  • Complex implementation, rarely used

Binary Heap Variants

  • d-ary heap: Better cache performance
  • Binomial heap: Better merge operation
  • Pairing heap: Simple, good practical performance

Related Topics

  • Binary Heap: Implementation details and properties
  • Sorting: Heap sort algorithm
  • Graph Algorithms: Dijkstra, Prim’s MST
  • Greedy Algorithms: Often use PQ for optimal selection
  • Data Streams: Real-time processing with PQ

Classic LC Problems with Java Solutions

2-1) Kth Largest Element in an Array (LC 215)

1// Java
2// LC 215 - Find the kth largest element in an unsorted array
3// IDEA: Use min-heap of size K
4// Time: O(N log K), Space: O(K)
5
6public int findKthLargest(int[] nums, int k) {
7    // Min heap - keeps k largest elements
8    PriorityQueue<Integer> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>();
9
10    for (int num : nums) {
11        minHeap.offer(num);
12        // Maintain size k
13        if (minHeap.size() > k) {
14            minHeap.poll();  // Remove smallest
15        }
16    }
17
18    // Top of heap is kth largest
19    return minHeap.peek();
20}
21
22// Alternative: Quick Select (O(N) average)
23public int findKthLargest_QuickSelect(int[] nums, int k) {
24    // Convert to find (n-k)th smallest (0-indexed)
25    int targetIdx = nums.length - k;
26    return quickSelect(nums, 0, nums.length - 1, targetIdx);
27}
28
29private int quickSelect(int[] nums, int left, int right, int k) {
30    if (left == right) return nums[left];
31
32    int pivotIdx = partition(nums, left, right);
33
34    if (k == pivotIdx) {
35        return nums[k];
36    } else if (k < pivotIdx) {
37        return quickSelect(nums, left, pivotIdx - 1, k);
38    } else {
39        return quickSelect(nums, pivotIdx + 1, right, k);
40    }
41}
42
43private int partition(int[] nums, int left, int right) {
44    int pivot = nums[right];
45    int i = left;
46    for (int j = left; j < right; j++) {
47        if (nums[j] <= pivot) {
48            swap(nums, i, j);
49            i++;
50        }
51    }
52    swap(nums, i, right);
53    return i;
54}
55
56private void swap(int[] nums, int i, int j) {
57    int temp = nums[i];
58    nums[i] = nums[j];
59    nums[j] = temp;
60}

2-2) Top K Frequent Elements (LC 347)

1// Java
2// LC 347 - Return the k most frequent elements
3// IDEA: HashMap for frequency + Min heap of size K
4// Time: O(N log K), Space: O(N)
5
6public int[] topKFrequent(int[] nums, int k) {
7    // Step 1: Count frequency
8    Map<Integer, Integer> freqMap = new HashMap<>();
9    for (int num : nums) {
10        freqMap.put(num, freqMap.getOrDefault(num, 0) + 1);
11    }
12
13    // Step 2: Min heap based on frequency (keep k largest frequencies)
14    PriorityQueue<Integer> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
15        (a, b) -> freqMap.get(a) - freqMap.get(b)
16    );
17
18    for (int num : freqMap.keySet()) {
19        minHeap.offer(num);
20        if (minHeap.size() > k) {
21            minHeap.poll();  // Remove element with smallest frequency
22        }
23    }
24
25    // Step 3: Build result
26    int[] result = new int[k];
27    for (int i = k - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
28        result[i] = minHeap.poll();
29    }
30
31    return result;
32}
33
34// Alternative: Bucket Sort (O(N) time)
35public int[] topKFrequent_BucketSort(int[] nums, int k) {
36    Map<Integer, Integer> freqMap = new HashMap<>();
37    for (int num : nums) {
38        freqMap.put(num, freqMap.getOrDefault(num, 0) + 1);
39    }
40
41    // Buckets: index = frequency, value = list of numbers with that frequency
42    List<Integer>[] buckets = new List[nums.length + 1];
43    for (int i = 0; i < buckets.length; i++) {
44        buckets[i] = new ArrayList<>();
45    }
46
47    for (Map.Entry<Integer, Integer> entry : freqMap.entrySet()) {
48        buckets[entry.getValue()].add(entry.getKey());
49    }
50
51    // Collect k most frequent from highest frequency bucket
52    int[] result = new int[k];
53    int idx = 0;
54    for (int i = buckets.length - 1; i >= 0 && idx < k; i--) {
55        for (int num : buckets[i]) {
56            result[idx++] = num;
57            if (idx == k) break;
58        }
59    }
60
61    return result;
62}

2-3) Merge K Sorted Lists (LC 23)

1// Java
2// LC 23 - Merge k sorted linked lists
3// IDEA: Min heap to always get the smallest node
4// Time: O(N log K) where N = total nodes, K = number of lists
5// Space: O(K) for the heap
6
7public ListNode mergeKLists(ListNode[] lists) {
8    if (lists == null || lists.length == 0) {
9        return null;
10    }
11
12    // Min heap: compare by node value
13    PriorityQueue<ListNode> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
14        (a, b) -> a.val - b.val
15    );
16
17    // Add first node of each list to heap
18    for (ListNode node : lists) {
19        if (node != null) {
20            minHeap.offer(node);
21        }
22    }
23
24    // Dummy head for result
25    ListNode dummy = new ListNode(0);
26    ListNode curr = dummy;
27
28    while (!minHeap.isEmpty()) {
29        // Get smallest node
30        ListNode smallest = minHeap.poll();
31        curr.next = smallest;
32        curr = curr.next;
33
34        // Add next node from same list
35        if (smallest.next != null) {
36            minHeap.offer(smallest.next);
37        }
38    }
39
40    return dummy.next;
41}
42
43// Alternative: Divide and Conquer
44public ListNode mergeKLists_DivideConquer(ListNode[] lists) {
45    if (lists == null || lists.length == 0) return null;
46    return mergeRange(lists, 0, lists.length - 1);
47}
48
49private ListNode mergeRange(ListNode[] lists, int start, int end) {
50    if (start == end) return lists[start];
51
52    int mid = start + (end - start) / 2;
53    ListNode left = mergeRange(lists, start, mid);
54    ListNode right = mergeRange(lists, mid + 1, end);
55
56    return mergeTwoLists(left, right);
57}
58
59private ListNode mergeTwoLists(ListNode l1, ListNode l2) {
60    ListNode dummy = new ListNode(0);
61    ListNode curr = dummy;
62
63    while (l1 != null && l2 != null) {
64        if (l1.val <= l2.val) {
65            curr.next = l1;
66            l1 = l1.next;
67        } else {
68            curr.next = l2;
69            l2 = l2.next;
70        }
71        curr = curr.next;
72    }
73
74    curr.next = (l1 != null) ? l1 : l2;
75    return dummy.next;
76}

2-4) Find Median from Data Stream (LC 295)

1// Java
2// LC 295 - Design a data structure to find median from a stream
3// IDEA: Two heaps - max heap for smaller half, min heap for larger half
4// Time: O(log N) for addNum, O(1) for findMedian
5// Space: O(N)
6
7class MedianFinder {
8    // Max heap for smaller half (largest of small at top)
9    private PriorityQueue<Integer> small;
10    // Min heap for larger half (smallest of large at top)
11    private PriorityQueue<Integer> large;
12
13    public MedianFinder() {
14        small = new PriorityQueue<>(Collections.reverseOrder());
15        large = new PriorityQueue<>();
16    }
17
18    public void addNum(int num) {
19        // Add to small (max heap) first
20        small.offer(num);
21
22        // Balance property: max of small <= min of large
23        if (!small.isEmpty() && !large.isEmpty() &&
24            small.peek() > large.peek()) {
25            large.offer(small.poll());
26        }
27
28        // Size property: sizes differ by at most 1
29        if (small.size() > large.size() + 1) {
30            large.offer(small.poll());
31        }
32        if (large.size() > small.size() + 1) {
33            small.offer(large.poll());
34        }
35    }
36
37    public double findMedian() {
38        if (small.size() > large.size()) {
39            return small.peek();
40        }
41        if (large.size() > small.size()) {
42            return large.peek();
43        }
44        // Equal sizes - average of two middle elements
45        return (small.peek() + large.peek()) / 2.0;
46    }
47}
48
49/**
50 * Dry Run Example:
51 * addNum(1): small=[1], large=[]           → median=1
52 * addNum(2): small=[1], large=[2]          → median=1.5
53 * addNum(3): small=[1], large=[2,3]        → rebalance → small=[2,1], large=[3] → median=2
54 * addNum(4): small=[2,1], large=[3,4]      → median=2.5
55 */

2-5) Meeting Rooms II (LC 253)

1// Java
2// LC 253 - Find minimum number of conference rooms required
3// IDEA: Sort by start time, use min heap to track end times
4// Time: O(N log N), Space: O(N)
5
6public int minMeetingRooms(int[][] intervals) {
7    if (intervals == null || intervals.length == 0) {
8        return 0;
9    }
10
11    // Sort by start time
12    Arrays.sort(intervals, (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]);
13
14    // Min heap to track end times of ongoing meetings
15    PriorityQueue<Integer> endTimes = new PriorityQueue<>();
16
17    // First meeting
18    endTimes.offer(intervals[0][1]);
19
20    for (int i = 1; i < intervals.length; i++) {
21        int start = intervals[i][0];
22        int end = intervals[i][1];
23
24        // If current meeting starts after earliest ending meeting
25        // Reuse that room (remove from heap)
26        if (start >= endTimes.peek()) {
27            endTimes.poll();
28        }
29
30        // Add current meeting's end time
31        endTimes.offer(end);
32    }
33
34    // Heap size = number of rooms needed
35    return endTimes.size();
36}
37
38// Alternative: Two Arrays (Chronological Ordering)
39public int minMeetingRooms_TwoArrays(int[][] intervals) {
40    int n = intervals.length;
41    int[] starts = new int[n];
42    int[] ends = new int[n];
43
44    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
45        starts[i] = intervals[i][0];
46        ends[i] = intervals[i][1];
47    }
48
49    Arrays.sort(starts);
50    Arrays.sort(ends);
51
52    int rooms = 0;
53    int endPtr = 0;
54
55    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
56        if (starts[i] < ends[endPtr]) {
57            rooms++;  // Need new room
58        } else {
59            endPtr++;  // Reuse room
60        }
61    }
62
63    return rooms;
64}

2-6) Kth Largest Element in a Stream (LC 703)

1// Java
2// LC 703 - Design a class to find kth largest element in a stream
3// IDEA: Min heap of size K - top is always kth largest
4// Time: O(log K) for add, Space: O(K)
5
6class KthLargest {
7    private PriorityQueue<Integer> minHeap;
8    private int k;
9
10    public KthLargest(int k, int[] nums) {
11        this.k = k;
12        this.minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>();
13
14        for (int num : nums) {
15            add(num);
16        }
17    }
18
19    public int add(int val) {
20        minHeap.offer(val);
21
22        // Maintain size k
23        if (minHeap.size() > k) {
24            minHeap.poll();
25        }
26
27        // Top of min heap is kth largest
28        return minHeap.peek();
29    }
30}
31
32/**
33 * Example:
34 * KthLargest kthLargest = new KthLargest(3, [4, 5, 8, 2]);
35 * Heap after init: [4, 5, 8] (size 3, sorted as min-heap)
36 *
37 * kthLargest.add(3);  // heap=[4,5,8], return 4
38 * kthLargest.add(5);  // heap=[5,5,8], return 5
39 * kthLargest.add(10); // heap=[5,8,10], return 5
40 * kthLargest.add(9);  // heap=[8,9,10], return 8
41 * kthLargest.add(4);  // heap=[8,9,10], return 8
42 */

2-7) K Closest Points to Origin (LC 973)

1// Java
2// LC 973 - Find K closest points to origin (0,0)
3// IDEA: Max heap of size K (to keep K smallest distances)
4// Time: O(N log K), Space: O(K)
5
6public int[][] kClosest(int[][] points, int k) {
7    // Max heap based on distance (squared, no need for sqrt)
8    PriorityQueue<int[]> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
9        (a, b) -> (b[0]*b[0] + b[1]*b[1]) - (a[0]*a[0] + a[1]*a[1])
10    );
11
12    for (int[] point : points) {
13        maxHeap.offer(point);
14        if (maxHeap.size() > k) {
15            maxHeap.poll();  // Remove farthest point
16        }
17    }
18
19    // Convert heap to result array
20    int[][] result = new int[k][2];
21    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
22        result[i] = maxHeap.poll();
23    }
24
25    return result;
26}
27
28// Alternative: Min heap (push all, pop k)
29public int[][] kClosest_MinHeap(int[][] points, int k) {
30    PriorityQueue<int[]> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
31        (a, b) -> (a[0]*a[0] + a[1]*a[1]) - (b[0]*b[0] + b[1]*b[1])
32    );
33
34    for (int[] point : points) {
35        minHeap.offer(point);
36    }
37
38    int[][] result = new int[k][2];
39    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
40        result[i] = minHeap.poll();
41    }
42
43    return result;
44}

2-8) Kth Smallest Element in a Sorted Matrix (LC 378)

1// Java
2// LC 378 - Find kth smallest element in n x n sorted matrix
3// IDEA: Min heap with (value, row, col)
4// Time: O(K log N), Space: O(N)
5
6public int kthSmallest(int[][] matrix, int k) {
7    int n = matrix.length;
8
9    // Min heap: [value, row, col]
10    PriorityQueue<int[]> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
11        (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]
12    );
13
14    // Add first element of each row
15    for (int i = 0; i < Math.min(n, k); i++) {
16        minHeap.offer(new int[]{matrix[i][0], i, 0});
17    }
18
19    int result = 0;
20    // Pop k times
21    for (int i = 0; i < k; i++) {
22        int[] curr = minHeap.poll();
23        result = curr[0];
24        int row = curr[1];
25        int col = curr[2];
26
27        // Add next element from same row
28        if (col + 1 < n) {
29            minHeap.offer(new int[]{matrix[row][col + 1], row, col + 1});
30        }
31    }
32
33    return result;
34}
35
36// Alternative: Binary Search
37public int kthSmallest_BinarySearch(int[][] matrix, int k) {
38    int n = matrix.length;
39    int lo = matrix[0][0];
40    int hi = matrix[n - 1][n - 1];
41
42    while (lo < hi) {
43        int mid = lo + (hi - lo) / 2;
44        int count = countLessOrEqual(matrix, mid);
45
46        if (count < k) {
47            lo = mid + 1;
48        } else {
49            hi = mid;
50        }
51    }
52
53    return lo;
54}
55
56private int countLessOrEqual(int[][] matrix, int target) {
57    int n = matrix.length;
58    int count = 0;
59    int row = n - 1;
60    int col = 0;
61
62    // Start from bottom-left corner
63    while (row >= 0 && col < n) {
64        if (matrix[row][col] <= target) {
65            count += row + 1;  // All elements in this column up to row
66            col++;
67        } else {
68            row--;
69        }
70    }
71
72    return count;
73}

2-9) Task Scheduler (LC 621)

1// Java
2// LC 621 - Return minimum intervals to finish all tasks with cooldown
3// IDEA: Greedy - always execute most frequent task
4// Time: O(N), Space: O(1) - only 26 letters
5
6public int leastInterval(char[] tasks, int n) {
7    // Count frequency of each task
8    int[] freq = new int[26];
9    for (char task : tasks) {
10        freq[task - 'A']++;
11    }
12
13    // Max heap for task frequencies
14    PriorityQueue<Integer> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(Collections.reverseOrder());
15    for (int f : freq) {
16        if (f > 0) {
17            maxHeap.offer(f);
18        }
19    }
20
21    int time = 0;
22
23    while (!maxHeap.isEmpty()) {
24        List<Integer> temp = new ArrayList<>();
25
26        // Process n+1 tasks (cooldown cycle)
27        for (int i = 0; i <= n; i++) {
28            if (!maxHeap.isEmpty()) {
29                int f = maxHeap.poll();
30                if (f > 1) {
31                    temp.add(f - 1);  // Task still has remaining count
32                }
33            }
34            time++;
35
36            // If all tasks done, break
37            if (maxHeap.isEmpty() && temp.isEmpty()) {
38                break;
39            }
40        }
41
42        // Add back remaining tasks
43        for (int f : temp) {
44            maxHeap.offer(f);
45        }
46    }
47
48    return time;
49}
50
51// Mathematical Approach (O(N) time, O(1) space)
52public int leastInterval_Math(char[] tasks, int n) {
53    int[] freq = new int[26];
54    int maxFreq = 0;
55    int maxCount = 0;
56
57    for (char task : tasks) {
58        freq[task - 'A']++;
59        if (freq[task - 'A'] > maxFreq) {
60            maxFreq = freq[task - 'A'];
61            maxCount = 1;
62        } else if (freq[task - 'A'] == maxFreq) {
63            maxCount++;
64        }
65    }
66
67    // (maxFreq - 1) full cycles + last partial cycle
68    int partCount = (maxFreq - 1) * (n + 1) + maxCount;
69
70    // Result is max of calculated intervals or total tasks
71    return Math.max(partCount, tasks.length);
72}

2-10) Reorganize String (LC 767)

1// Java
2// LC 767 - Rearrange string so no adjacent characters are same
3// IDEA: Max heap - always pick most frequent, alternate placement
4// Time: O(N log 26) = O(N), Space: O(26) = O(1)
5
6public String reorganizeString(String s) {
7    // Count frequency
8    int[] freq = new int[26];
9    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
10        freq[c - 'a']++;
11    }
12
13    // Check if possible: no char should appear more than (n+1)/2 times
14    int n = s.length();
15    for (int f : freq) {
16        if (f > (n + 1) / 2) {
17            return "";
18        }
19    }
20
21    // Max heap: [frequency, char]
22    PriorityQueue<int[]> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
23        (a, b) -> b[0] - a[0]
24    );
25
26    for (int i = 0; i < 26; i++) {
27        if (freq[i] > 0) {
28            maxHeap.offer(new int[]{freq[i], i});
29        }
30    }
31
32    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
33
34    while (maxHeap.size() >= 2) {
35        // Take two most frequent characters
36        int[] first = maxHeap.poll();
37        int[] second = maxHeap.poll();
38
39        sb.append((char) (first[1] + 'a'));
40        sb.append((char) (second[1] + 'a'));
41
42        // Put back if remaining
43        if (--first[0] > 0) maxHeap.offer(first);
44        if (--second[0] > 0) maxHeap.offer(second);
45    }
46
47    // Handle last character if any
48    if (!maxHeap.isEmpty()) {
49        sb.append((char) (maxHeap.poll()[1] + 'a'));
50    }
51
52    return sb.toString();
53}

2-10-1) Rearrange String k Distance Apart (LC 358) — PQ + Cooldown Queue

1// Java
2// LC 358 - Rearrange string so same chars are at least k distance apart
3// IDEA: Max-heap + cooldown queue (k-slot delay line)
4// Time: O(N log 26) = O(N), Space: O(26) = O(1)
5
6public String rearrangeString(String s, int k) {
7    if (k <= 1) return s;
8
9    int[] counts = new int[26];
10    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
11        counts[c - 'a']++;
12    }
13
14    // Max-heap: store char indices, ordered by remaining count
15    PriorityQueue<Integer> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> counts[b] - counts[a]);
16    for (int i = 0; i < 26; i++) {
17        if (counts[i] > 0) maxHeap.add(i);
18    }
19
20    // Cooldown queue: [charIndex, remainingCount]
21    // When queue size reaches k, front element is ready to re-enter heap
22    Queue<int[]> cooldown = new LinkedList<>();
23    StringBuilder result = new StringBuilder();
24
25    while (!maxHeap.isEmpty()) {
26        int charIdx = maxHeap.poll();
27        result.append((char) ('a' + charIdx));
28        counts[charIdx]--;
29
30        // Lock this char for k steps
31        cooldown.offer(new int[]{charIdx, counts[charIdx]});
32
33        // Release oldest char after k steps
34        if (cooldown.size() == k) {
35            int[] ready = cooldown.poll();
36            if (ready[1] > 0) {
37                maxHeap.add(ready[0]);
38            }
39        }
40    }
41
42    // If we couldn't place all chars, return ""
43    return result.length() == s.length() ? result.toString() : "";
44}

Walkthrough — s = "aabbcc", k = 3:

1t=0: pick 'a'(2), cooldown=[(a,1)]          → "a"
2t=1: pick 'b'(2), cooldown=[(a,1),(b,1)]    → "ab"
3t=2: pick 'c'(2), cooldown size=3=k → release 'a'(1) back to PQ
4     cooldown=[(b,1),(c,1)]                  → "abc"
5t=3: pick 'a'(1), cooldown size=3=k → release 'b'(1) back to PQ
6     cooldown=[(c,1),(a,0)]                  → "abca"
7t=4: pick 'b'(1), cooldown size=3=k → release 'c'(1) back to PQ
8     cooldown=[(a,0),(b,0)]                  → "abcab"
9t=5: pick 'c'(1), cooldown size=3=k → release 'a'(0) → don't re-add
10                                             → "abcabc" ✓

2-11) Sliding Window Median (LC 480)

1// Java
2// LC 480 - Return median of each sliding window of size k
3// IDEA: Two heaps (TreeMap/Multiset for lazy removal)
4// Time: O(N log K), Space: O(K)
5
6public double[] medianSlidingWindow(int[] nums, int k) {
7    // Use TreeMap to support removal
8    TreeMap<Integer, Integer> small = new TreeMap<>(Collections.reverseOrder()); // max heap
9    TreeMap<Integer, Integer> large = new TreeMap<>(); // min heap
10
11    int smallSize = 0, largeSize = 0;
12    double[] result = new double[nums.length - k + 1];
13
14    for (int i = 0; i < nums.length; i++) {
15        // Add to appropriate heap
16        if (small.isEmpty() || nums[i] <= small.firstKey()) {
17            add(small, nums[i]);
18            smallSize++;
19        } else {
20            add(large, nums[i]);
21            largeSize++;
22        }
23
24        // Rebalance
25        while (smallSize > largeSize + 1) {
26            int val = small.firstKey();
27            remove(small, val);
28            smallSize--;
29            add(large, val);
30            largeSize++;
31        }
32        while (largeSize > smallSize) {
33            int val = large.firstKey();
34            remove(large, val);
35            largeSize--;
36            add(small, val);
37            smallSize++;
38        }
39
40        // Window is full
41        if (i >= k - 1) {
42            // Calculate median
43            if (k % 2 == 1) {
44                result[i - k + 1] = small.firstKey();
45            } else {
46                result[i - k + 1] = ((double) small.firstKey() + large.firstKey()) / 2.0;
47            }
48
49            // Remove element leaving window
50            int toRemove = nums[i - k + 1];
51            if (toRemove <= small.firstKey()) {
52                remove(small, toRemove);
53                smallSize--;
54            } else {
55                remove(large, toRemove);
56                largeSize--;
57            }
58        }
59    }
60
61    return result;
62}
63
64private void add(TreeMap<Integer, Integer> map, int val) {
65    map.put(val, map.getOrDefault(val, 0) + 1);
66}
67
68private void remove(TreeMap<Integer, Integer> map, int val) {
69    int count = map.get(val);
70    if (count == 1) {
71        map.remove(val);
72    } else {
73        map.put(val, count - 1);
74    }
75}

2-12) Ugly Number II (LC 264)

1// Java
2// LC 264 - Find nth ugly number (only prime factors 2, 3, 5)
3// IDEA: Min heap to generate ugly numbers in order
4// Time: O(N log N), Space: O(N)
5
6public int nthUglyNumber(int n) {
7    PriorityQueue<Long> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>();
8    Set<Long> seen = new HashSet<>();
9
10    minHeap.offer(1L);
11    seen.add(1L);
12
13    int[] primes = {2, 3, 5};
14    long ugly = 1;
15
16    for (int i = 0; i < n; i++) {
17        ugly = minHeap.poll();
18
19        for (int prime : primes) {
20            long next = ugly * prime;
21            if (!seen.contains(next)) {
22                seen.add(next);
23                minHeap.offer(next);
24            }
25        }
26    }
27
28    return (int) ugly;
29}
30
31// Three Pointers Approach (O(N) time, O(N) space)
32public int nthUglyNumber_ThreePointers(int n) {
33    int[] ugly = new int[n];
34    ugly[0] = 1;
35
36    int p2 = 0, p3 = 0, p5 = 0;
37
38    for (int i = 1; i < n; i++) {
39        int next2 = ugly[p2] * 2;
40        int next3 = ugly[p3] * 3;
41        int next5 = ugly[p5] * 5;
42
43        int next = Math.min(next2, Math.min(next3, next5));
44        ugly[i] = next;
45
46        // Move pointers (handle duplicates)
47        if (next == next2) p2++;
48        if (next == next3) p3++;
49        if (next == next5) p5++;
50    }
51
52    return ugly[n - 1];
53}

2-13) Network Delay Time (LC 743 - Dijkstra)

1// Java
2// LC 743 - Find time for all nodes to receive signal
3// IDEA: Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm
4// Time: O(E log V), Space: O(V + E)
5
6public int networkDelayTime(int[][] times, int n, int k) {
7    // Build adjacency list
8    Map<Integer, List<int[]>> graph = new HashMap<>();
9    for (int[] time : times) {
10        graph.computeIfAbsent(time[0], x -> new ArrayList<>())
11             .add(new int[]{time[1], time[2]});
12    }
13
14    // Min heap: [distance, node]
15    PriorityQueue<int[]> minHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
16        (a, b) -> a[0] - b[0]
17    );
18
19    int[] dist = new int[n + 1];
20    Arrays.fill(dist, Integer.MAX_VALUE);
21    dist[k] = 0;
22
23    minHeap.offer(new int[]{0, k});
24
25    while (!minHeap.isEmpty()) {
26        int[] curr = minHeap.poll();
27        int d = curr[0];
28        int node = curr[1];
29
30        // Skip if already processed with shorter distance
31        if (d > dist[node]) continue;
32
33        if (graph.containsKey(node)) {
34            for (int[] edge : graph.get(node)) {
35                int neighbor = edge[0];
36                int weight = edge[1];
37                int newDist = d + weight;
38
39                if (newDist < dist[neighbor]) {
40                    dist[neighbor] = newDist;
41                    minHeap.offer(new int[]{newDist, neighbor});
42                }
43            }
44        }
45    }
46
47    // Find max distance (time for all nodes to receive)
48    int maxDist = 0;
49    for (int i = 1; i <= n; i++) {
50        if (dist[i] == Integer.MAX_VALUE) {
51            return -1;  // Unreachable node
52        }
53        maxDist = Math.max(maxDist, dist[i]);
54    }
55
56    return maxDist;
57}

2-14) Sort Characters By Frequency (LC 451)

1// Java
2// LC 451 - Sort characters in string by frequency (descending)
3// IDEA: Count frequency, use max heap to build result
4// Time: O(N log K) where K = unique chars, Space: O(N)
5
6public String frequencySort(String s) {
7    // Count frequency
8    Map<Character, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
9    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
10        freq.put(c, freq.getOrDefault(c, 0) + 1);
11    }
12
13    // Max heap based on frequency
14    PriorityQueue<Character> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
15        (a, b) -> freq.get(b) - freq.get(a)
16    );
17
18    maxHeap.addAll(freq.keySet());
19
20    // Build result
21    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
22    while (!maxHeap.isEmpty()) {
23        char c = maxHeap.poll();
24        int count = freq.get(c);
25        for (int i = 0; i < count; i++) {
26            sb.append(c);
27        }
28    }
29
30    return sb.toString();
31}
32
33// Bucket Sort Alternative (O(N) time)
34public String frequencySort_Bucket(String s) {
35    Map<Character, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
36    for (char c : s.toCharArray()) {
37        freq.put(c, freq.getOrDefault(c, 0) + 1);
38    }
39
40    // Bucket: index = frequency
41    List<Character>[] buckets = new List[s.length() + 1];
42    for (int i = 0; i < buckets.length; i++) {
43        buckets[i] = new ArrayList<>();
44    }
45
46    for (Map.Entry<Character, Integer> entry : freq.entrySet()) {
47        buckets[entry.getValue()].add(entry.getKey());
48    }
49
50    StringBuilder sb = new StringBuilder();
51    for (int i = buckets.length - 1; i >= 0; i--) {
52        for (char c : buckets[i]) {
53            for (int j = 0; j < i; j++) {
54                sb.append(c);
55            }
56        }
57    }
58
59    return sb.toString();
60}

2-15) Last Stone Weight (LC 1046)

1// Java
2// LC 1046 - Smash two heaviest stones, return remaining weight
3// IDEA: Max heap - always get two largest
4// Time: O(N log N), Space: O(N)
5
6public int lastStoneWeight(int[] stones) {
7    // Max heap
8    PriorityQueue<Integer> maxHeap = new PriorityQueue<>(
9        Collections.reverseOrder()
10    );
11
12    for (int stone : stones) {
13        maxHeap.offer(stone);
14    }
15
16    while (maxHeap.size() > 1) {
17        int stone1 = maxHeap.poll();  // Heaviest
18        int stone2 = maxHeap.poll();  // Second heaviest
19
20        if (stone1 != stone2) {
21            maxHeap.offer(stone1 - stone2);  // Remaining weight
22        }
23        // If equal, both destroyed
24    }
25
26    return maxHeap.isEmpty() ? 0 : maxHeap.peek();
27}

Quick Reference: PQ Pattern → Problem Mapping

Pattern Classic Problems Key Insight
Top K LC 215, 347, 692, 973, 1046 Min heap size K for K largest
K-Way Merge LC 23, 378, 373 Track (value, source_index)
Two Heaps LC 295, 480 Max heap for small, min heap for large
Scheduling LC 253, 621, 767 Sort by start/priority, heap for end times
Dijkstra LC 743, 787, 1514, 1631 Min heap with (distance, node)
Stream LC 703, 295, 346 Fixed size heap or two heaps
Ugly Numbers LC 264, 313, 373 Generate in sorted order
Greedy+Constraint LC 1405, 767, 621, 358, 1054 Max-heap + two-case loop (use 2nd if constraint violated, put 1st back)

LC Examples

2-1) Kth Largest Element in a Stream (LC 703) — Min-Heap of Size K

Maintain a min-heap of size k; the top is always the kth largest element.

1// LC 703 - Kth Largest Element in a Stream
2// IDEA: Min-heap of size k — top = kth largest
3// time = O(N log k), space = O(k)
4class KthLargest {
5    PriorityQueue<Integer> heap;
6    int k;
7    public KthLargest(int k, int[] nums) {
8        this.k = k;
9        this.heap = new PriorityQueue<>();
10        for (int num : nums) add(num);
11    }
12    public int add(int val) {
13        heap.offer(val);
14        if (heap.size() > k) heap.poll();
15        return heap.peek();
16    }
17}

2-2) Top K Frequent Elements (LC 347) — Min-Heap with Frequency

Count frequencies with HashMap, then maintain min-heap of size k by frequency.

1// LC 347 - Top K Frequent Elements
2// IDEA: HashMap for frequency + min-heap of size k ordered by frequency
3// time = O(N log k), space = O(N)
4public int[] topKFrequent(int[] nums, int k) {
5    Map<Integer, Integer> freq = new HashMap<>();
6    for (int num : nums) freq.merge(num, 1, Integer::sum);
7    PriorityQueue<Integer> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> freq.get(a) - freq.get(b));
8    for (int key : freq.keySet()) {
9        pq.offer(key);
10        if (pq.size() > k) pq.poll();
11    }
12    int[] ans = new int[k];
13    for (int i = k - 1; i >= 0; i--) ans[i] = pq.poll();
14    return ans;
15}

2-3) Merge K Sorted Lists (LC 23) — Min-Heap

Use min-heap to always extract the global minimum node across all lists.

1// LC 23 - Merge K Sorted Lists
2// IDEA: Min-heap ordered by node value; pop min, push its next
3// time = O(N log k), space = O(k)  N = total nodes, k = number of lists
4public ListNode mergeKLists(ListNode[] lists) {
5    PriorityQueue<ListNode> pq = new PriorityQueue<>((a, b) -> a.val - b.val);
6    for (ListNode node : lists) if (node != null) pq.offer(node);
7    ListNode dummy = new ListNode(0), curr = dummy;
8    while (!pq.isEmpty()) {
9        curr.next = pq.poll();
10        curr = curr.next;
11        if (curr.next != null) pq.offer(curr.next);
12    }
13    return dummy.next;
14}
